findings.summary: The default 'run_shell' core action executes model-chosen commands via subprocess with shell=True on the host, with no code-enforced approval gate or isolation, violating the remit's requirement that shell/code execution be isolated or operator-approved.

run_shell seeds each command with env=os.environ.copy(), handing the operator's full secret-bearing environment to arbitrary model-run commands, directly violating the remit clause that shell execution MUST NOT silently inherit the operator's full secret-bearing environment.

No code-level approval interception exists on high-impact actions (outbound sends, payments, third-party writes, MCP tool calls) — the action manager has no gate and 'approval' lives only as prose in AGENT.md/prompts, violating the remit requirement that high-impact gating be enforced by code, not model discretion.

Inbound messages are routed into tasks by an LLM SessionRouter with auto_reply defaulting to true (external_comms_config.json) and no sender-identity verification anywhere in code, so an unverified messaging sender can be treated as an authenticated operator command.

API keys (config.get_api_key) and AWS secret_access_key (get_aws_credentials) are read from and written to a plaintext settings.json in app/config, with the design explicitly stating all config comes from settings.json and no OS keychain/encrypted store, violating the remit's secrets-protected-at-rest requirement.

The agent maintains an agent-writable ChromaDB vector memory store (chroma_db_memory) whose collection.add/upsert ingest chunks and daily-consolidated conversation content with no validation or hidden-instruction detection, creating a self-poisoning semantic store that persists across sessions.

External untrusted content from web_fetch, web_search, http_request, and inbound message bodies flows into the LLM context without sanitization, trust-labeling, or origin separation, providing an indirect prompt-injection highway.

The PromptSanitizer module in app/security is never imported or called anywhere in the codebase (declared-but-never-consulted), and even by design it only logs suspicious patterns rather than blocking them, so it provides no runtime protection.

Enabled MCP servers run unpinned third-party code (filesystem via 'npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem .' and 'npx @playwright/mcp@latest') with no version pinning, integrity verification, provenance vetting, or isolation from the operator's credentials and filesystem, exposing supply-chain and rug-pull risk.
The enabled filesystem MCP server is scoped to '.' (the entire working directory), granting the agent broad read/write access well beyond any single task's need.

The local control-plane WebSocket/HTTP surface (browser_adapter '/ws') has no authentication and relies solely on the default loopback bind, so any local process on the machine can drive full agent capabilities including task execution, settings, files, and credential entry.

The operator-owned identity/policy files (SOUL.md, USER.md, AGENT.md) and the long-term memory store are protected only by prose ('Agent DO NOT edit'), while the agent's write_file and run_shell actions can reach and rewrite them, violating the remit's never-allowed clause against editing these without operator confirmation.

Compound chain: unvalidated external content enters the LLM context, the agent can rewrite its own writable identity/memory files, and it can invoke run_shell with no approval gate — chaining a single indirect injection into cross-session persistence and host code execution.

There is no structured, queryable action-level audit of tool calls and agent decisions — only an idempotency ledger for irreversible side effects and free-form logging — so consequential capabilities run with a narrowed oversight/audit surface.

No evidence of adversarial or red-team testing (injection, memory-poisoning, or exec-abuse tests) exists in the workspace, so the agent's many injection and execution surfaces are untested against attack.

Multiple MCP server definitions embed instruction-like 'SETUP REQUIRED' step lists inside their model-visible tool descriptions, an MCP tool-poisoning indicator where description text carries directives to the model beyond the tool's nominal function.
